sentences.text: Ordinary school, yes.
Ordinary state school.
I went to the-- the ordinary school [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after the occupation.
And then at one time-- and I can't remember exactly the year-- we were not allowed to go to the normal school anymore.
Where at that time, after that time, in the new area where we had moved to, I used to go with my [? boy ?] friend-- Jopi was his name.
We used to go together, he and I, and my sister, my sister being one year younger than I.
We went-- every morning, we walked and went to that school.
But after the occupation-- as I said, I don't remember which year-- we were stopped going to that school.
Correct.
He was not Jewish.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
My parents-- my father was somewhat befriended with his father.
And his father and mother had only the one son, my friend Jopi.
My parents used to visit each other.
And there was a time after the liberation-- after the occupation that we had to give in our radios.
We were not allowed to have a radio anymore.
So my father and his father swapped radios, because we had, at that time, a what supposedly was a nice Philips radio, and they had a lesser radio.
So my father swapped his radio for Jopi's father's radio so that in that way, my father gave the lesser radio to the Germans, as was required by that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So in that way, I think my parents got somewhat closer to my friend's parents.
Because they still clandestinely listened to the BBC, and they would tell my parents what the BBC had said on the radio, which was very dangerous to do for them.
But at that time, we no longer had a radio, because we were not allowed to have a radio.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, there came a time when the Dutch people in general had to give up any gold that they had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the Jewish people had to give in the bicycles.
And this was a big blow to me, because I lost my bicycle.
My father had to take his and mine to a place near where we lived to give the bicycles.
Because in that day, we didn't have a car.
We had bicycles.
As so many people in Holland had, and still have.
Never.
Never.
Never.

I-- my sister really was-- whenever she was outside the home with young people, she was either at school with all kinds of back people-- youngsters of different backgrounds, or she was with me and my friends.
She was more with me, I would say, than on her own with her friends outside of school.
It is difficult after so many years to be exact about the closeness that we may have had at that time.
I know that as the war progressed, the closeness became much more extreme than what I think it was in the early days of the occupation, or indeed before the occupation.
But she played with us always at home with my mother.
Because we were, as Jewish children, through the laws of that time, being more and more separated from our friends, who were also Jewish.
But I mean by that more our non-Jewish friends.
So we become more and more closer to the Jewish boys and girls and to each other.
No, I do not.
I don't remember her complaining.
I don't remember my parents complaining about-- they may indeed have done that to other adults.
But never to me as a child.
It's difficult for me to-- not to remember, but to think that I was a child those days.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
It singled us out because, [? hell, ?] the things that I've already told you.
Also, that the-- the adults had to have an identity card which had a big 'J' stamp next to their photograph.
In Dutch, it was known as a [DUTCH].
And of course, it singled us out.
From before the war, no Christmas trees.
[LAUGHS] Then stop being having to go-- being able to go to school and having to go to school, as was the law.
No more bicycles.

And then, of course, as the time went on, my parents-- I heard my parents talking that people had been put on 'transport'-- that's the-- that's the literal translation of the Dutch word, which is very similar-- and had been sent away.
Or the word was called-- another word that was used-- Dutch word was [DUTCH].
That Dutch means 'collected.' And another place that was identified and one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] obtained knowledge from was the [DUTCH].

The [DUTCH] was before the war, a theater [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that became a place where the Jews were picked up on the [DUTCH] taken to, kept in this [DUTCH], in the theater, until they were transported to the east.

I, as a child at that time, thought, especially when my [SIGHS] grandparents were gone and my other family-- I thought they'd been taken to places in Germany in east-- because Germany is east from Holland-- to work.
And that's all I knew.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And as the time progressed, we were becoming-- or I was becoming-- more and more aware that more and more people were gone.
I would see less children than what I played with it.
My mother or father would say, oh the [DUTCH], this and this and this went yesterday, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without having to say anymore, 'went' meant they're not there anymore.
In their house or whatever, anymore.
So I became, as the time progressed, more and more aware that people were not there anymore.
I don't really want to use the word 'disappearing.' They were-- yes, they were disappearing.
But they were not there anymore.
So as the time progressed, I became more and more aware that [SIGHS] we were different.
Did you think that's going to happen to you, too?
I didn't really think it was going to happen to me.
Because I'd always felt from my mother, because she was English by birth, there would be special consideration given to us.
Because we were half-English.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother, who had one sister, also living in Amsterdam, married to a Dutchman, with a child, my cousin-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she and he survived.
And we-- my mother and she thought we would be somehow interned, or something.
We didn't think that we would go away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That became more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lesser possibility when all of a sudden, we were commanded to leave our home where we were.
And we were forcibly moved to another part of Amsterdam, to what was, in actual fact, a ghetto, one of the parts of Amsterdam which they had proclaimed as a [GERMAN].
We had to leave our home and go there.
Then things started, for me, to change a lot.
And for my family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was away from my neighborhood.
It was away from my friend Jopi.
It was away from everything that had become, shall I say, my life.
Down the street where we lived, there was a swimming pool which I used to go to.
There was open land where we used to play football.
And then I was taken-- we were taken-- to the eastern part of Amsterdam, which is known as the-- in Dutch, it's called-- called [DUTCH].
It is-- it is this part of Amsterdam where all the streets and everything are named after South Africa.
All the names of the streets.
And we were moved to a place, a street.
And the address is-- was 60 [DUTCH].
The [DUTCH].
[DUTCH] is the name of a man-- a Dutch settler of the 1600s who went to South Africa.
South Africa was Dutch originally.
So all the streets are-- are Dutch names, but refer to places in South Africa.
And we lived in that street.
And in that street and in that building, that house-- it was a house with apartments, and we lived on the third floor of that apartment.
I personally knew nobody there.
It was a completely other area of Amsterdam, which I-- I don't think I'd ever been to before.
And there were non-Jewish people living there in that area.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know-- I can't remember how long it was that we moved from south Amsterdam to the east of Amsterdam to the Jewish quarter.
And I don't remember how long I lived in that Jewish quarter until we also were deported from there.
No, I don't remember him going to visit me.
But I remember meeting him on the river-- on the River [?
Amstel, ?] which wasn't very far from where we lived then, to go fishing.
Because it wasn't a closed ghetto.
It was just-- I mean, you weren't allowed on the streets after a certain time in the evening.
Nobody was allowed on the streets, in the whole-- whether you were Jewish or not, after a certain time in the evening.
But it was not a closed ghetto.
There were no-- it wasn't a ghetto as such.
It was just an area which was deemed as a Jewish quarter.
But I didn't mean to say there were no non-Jews there.
Non-Jews lived there as well.
No, I do not remember whether we had any conversation about that.
No.
No, I don't remember that.
I don't remember that.
I do recall that his father came to see my father.
Because at that time, I think my mother at that time started to give Jopi's father and mother things to keep.
It was at that time that my mother and father must have realized that it was only a matter of time before we would be sent to the east.
So my mother started giving photographs of the family.

And some letters, silver, and stuff that my mother had had from her mother, from her brothers and sisters, and indeed, from my father's parents, my mother started to give to Jopi's father to take home to keep.
Do you remember whether your parents had correspondence with your grandmother in England?
I don't recall [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at that time that my mother had correspondence with her mother.
But I do recall that there was correspondence through the Red Cross.
There were no tele-- we didn't have a telephone.
There were no telephone conversations, as we didn't have a telephone.
That wouldn't have been allowed.
But we did-- my mother did write to my grandmother through the Red Cross.
The reason I know that is because of the two letters that were given to Jopi's father, which we got back after the war.
Those two letters are in the Washington Museum.
I donated them.
They were special form letter both in German and Dutch.
One side was written by the sender.
The other side was written by the return addressee.
So the letter has both-- it's one piece of paper, both sides.
I have photocopies, but the originals are in Washington.
Where my mother wrote to her mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And my-- my grandmother wrote back to my mother in Dutch.
Because that was my grandmother's language, really.
Because she had come from Holland, of course, many years before the war.
That was the only correspondence.
And the correspondence consists of about, you know, three very short-- four short sentences. 'We are well.
Kisses from the children.' That's all.
That's all.
Because all that mail must have been censored, I'm sure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the only-- that's the only contact my parents and my mother, in this instance, had with her family in-- in England.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other thing my mother started to do when we were living in the Jewish quarter-- [SIGHS] my friend's father had gotten for my parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cloth of a khaki color.
And from that, my mother made a rucksack for each of us-- for my sister, and my father, and my mother, myself.
And also a shoulder bag of the same material.
My mother had made that.
The-- also-- other thing my mother did was, in retrospect, very important.
Although at that time, I didn't know why I needed to know.
She make my sister and I memorize [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the address of my-- [SOBBING] of my grandmother in England.
We memorized that.
And of course, I know it to this day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the time, I didn't see why we needed to know that.
But in actual fact, as time progressed, it was important that I did know it.
So she made us memorize the mother's address, the grandmother's address.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember how long we lived in the Jewish quarter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember that middle of the night when they came to fetch us.
That, I do remember.
No.
I had no-- from what I recall, I had no friends there.
No.
At this time, I was about 10-- 10 and a bit--
10 And a bit.
My sister, a year younger than me, nine and a bit.
The-- my mother or father must have woken us up in the middle of night-- about 3 o'clock.
I don't know if it was 3 o'clock, but it was in the middle of the night.
And she said we had to get dressed.
And I remember we had-- my mother made us put on double underwear, double socks, and pack our rucksacks.
And I remember there was a lot of shouting in the street, and lights.
And all kinds of vehicles.
And and what they had in Germany, the Germans, was the Grune Polizei, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all in green.
And they come up the stairs with guns.
And they-- and they made us get out of the house.
And of course, in Holland, those old houses have very high stairs-- very steep and very narrow.
So it's not easy to quickly go down.
It's much easier for me at that age, then, than what it may have been for my parents or indeed, much older people.
Very dangerous to go fast down the stairs.
And they made us get out and stand outside, all-- all-- all in groups of people outside the house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in-- in the summertime.
It wasn't cold.
And they made us march to a station, which I've never been to before.
Because it was a completely, shall I say, not unknown area of Amsterdam, but not an area where I had frequented before.
So I didn't really know if there was a station, or where I was.
And we went to-- to the station, which is called the [DUTCH], which is still there today-- the station.
And we were standing on the platforms for a long time, I think until daybreak.
And then we were all put in-- in-- in cattle wagons at the station.
My mother and father, and my sister and I, and other people.
But no one else that I knew at that time.
We were cramped in there.
And the train went on its way, but we didn't know to where.
I didn't know where-- to where.
My parents may have known-- I don't know.
Other people may have known.
I don't remember how long the train journey was.
It may have been the rest of that day, which was that-- in the morning, we got into the train.
It may have taken the rest of that day.
I don't know whether it took an overnight.
Although the distance to where it eventually went, I wouldn't have thought it would have taken more than one day.
But I don't know.
And we then arrived in the Dutch camp in the province of Drenthe-- northeast Holland, a very bleak area, which-- where I'd never been.
I knew from school the name of the province.
And we arrived at the camp named Westerbork.
The Grune Polizei-- were those Germans?
Yes.
They were all Germans.
All Germans, spoke German.
I didn't speak German then.
I remember, you know, a lot of shouting in-- in a language that, at that time, I didn't know.
I may have understood some words possibly, because German and Dutch are similar, although not the same.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I just followed what my parents told me to do.

Well, I knew before that time-- my parents either had told me, or I'd overheard them talking-- that there were a lot of Dutch collaborators-- many, many Dutch collaborators that had joined the what was known in Holland, [DUTCH], which is NSB.
It stands for [DUTCH].
They-- and their colors-- they had a uniform-- and their colors were black and red.
And a lot of the Dutch people joined.
As a matter of fact, my non-Jewish friend, Jopi-- one of his uncles had joined.
That, he told me-- that he knew that one of his uncles had joined.
But in the people that came to fetch us, the Grune Polizei-- I'm not aware now, or indeed I don't think I was aware then, that there were Dutch amongst them.
There may very well and certainly could have been.
I don't-- we don't-- I don't know.
My father's parents had gone.
About a year and a half.
I don't-- I don't know.
I didn't know then when they left, and I don't really know the exact date now when they left.
My father, and maybe even my mother, possibly knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember one time going with my father.
[SIGHS] Near to us lived an aunt.
Near to us lived an aunt of ours who was the sister of my father's father.
So she was an aunt to me.
Because in Holland, those days, and still today, young people were very respectful of their elders, and families.
And everybody-- even although they may not have been, which is not the case in this instance-- everybody was [DUTCH] and [DUTCH].
There was no such a thing as 'you,' 'you,' 'you.' It was [DUTCH].
So I remember one day, my father took me, and he said to me, [SPEAKING DUTCH].
In other words, he said to me, we'll go and have a look if Auntie Elizabeth is at home.
And I remember very distinctly we went to where she lived.
And she didn't answer the door.
And then we knocked on the neighbors.
And the Dutch-- Dutch lady, of course-- a non-Jewish lady-- says, [SPEAKING DUTCH].
Her name was Elizabeth Levine, which is like in English, 'Leh-veen' or 'Leh-vine.' She said, no.
[SPEAKING DUTCH].
Translated, means Mrs.
Levine was 'collected'-- taken away yesterday.
And this was my aunt.
And, of course, the father-- the mother of one of my father's first cousins.
Yes, collected by the Germans.
Collected by the Grune Polizei.
Put on transport to the [INAUDIBLE], to east.
That's what I knew.
No.
No.
No.
No.
My mother-- my father did tell me that there were lots of German Jews that come to Holland, because they thought they would be safe in Holland.
Because in Holland, as it had been neutral in World War I, when things were going on in Germany, nobody thought it would ever happen in Holland.
And people in Holland, the Jewish people and the non-Jewish people, were not afraid, because they never thought it would happen.
And they said, 'it can never happen here.' They really believed it could never happen here.
I can remember myself being in the cattle car.
There were a lot of us in there.
I know in the corner was a barrel for-- to do whatever you had to do.
And I know there was a little bit of straw on the ground.
As far as I recall, it was cramped.
It was-- almost standing room only.
But as children, I sat between the people's legs.
And that is basically all I can remember.
I know I was with my mother and father, my sister close by.
I don't recall anybody else's name or anything like that in there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that is basically what I-- only what I recall.
Because I think the journey was comparatively short because of the physical distance between Amsterdam and Westerbork is not that great, as Holland is a small country.
No.
